author: nero_eth
content:
+1, let's make the timeout shorter.
The 1s timeout has been particularly stressful for min-bid users. They wait 1s for the bid while stopping iterating on the locally built block at 0s, wasting 1s for nothing.
Since timeouts are sent to the relay, sophisticated proposer can increase that value, making the relay delay the response, but at least they can't for "honest" proposers who just want the response immediately.

author: tersec
content: In which case, see the ping times linked. I'm, again, not claiming they're necessarily authoritative, accurate, or true. But just deciding to aggressively go for 200ms (or even 300ms) is questionable

author: tersec
content: Whatever the ping times from/to Auckland (or other similar places) are, they should not but cut off from this API for the sake of aggressive timeouts

author: tersec
content: And <@755590043632140352> handwaved 200ms to start with, so it's not as if that number has salient provenance, he just wants it fast

author: m.kalinin
content:
I have a question on testing related to ePBS.

This PR: https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/pull/5449 touches payload generation for the genesis block and as far as i can get from the existing design, there was a convention to leave genesis block an empty block and make its bid refer to a "genesis" payload. In other words, genesis block is empty but it is "built" on top of a payload that is kind of "genesis" payload. The PR doesn't change this behavior, it just changes the `parent_block_hash` in the genesis block bid.

I want to confirm if my understanding on how to properly handle genesis block payload in tests is correct. cc <@586161934425128960> <@755590043632140352>

author: nero_eth
content: yeah not saying to go for 200ms. I'd keep it rather conservative. Maybe 400 or 500ms

author: potuz
content: We've merged 300ms for Gloas, happy to change when there's any consensus on clients but it obviously looks like something that will be dragged forever so we just decided to merge and hope that people will achieve a reasonable consensus

author: potuz
content: <@654267572107083777> notice that for a proposer in the middle of nowhere, contacting external builders makes their blocks even more likely to be dropped.

author: potuz
content:
Take a look at this thread 
https://discord.com/channels/595666850260713488/874767108809031740/1501454580389642240

author: nflaig
content: Setting this to 300ms doesn't sound reasonable to me, that just hurts nodes at the edge of the network. I do think we can lower the value but it's also not clear to me what the goal is here, proposers are already side stepping the current 1 second timeout by just calling produce block later into the slot and playing timing games that way. What I never liked about the 1 second was that it delayed the local block publishing in case the timeout was triggered and increased risk of orphaning the block, but even that becomes less critical with gloas since you only need to propagate the tiny beacon block now before the attestation deadline. I could see us lowering this to ~500ms, or maybe even keep it out of the spec and let clients decide what value to use here, as mentioned above `X-Timeout-MS` could help so builders know what timeout to expect.

author: tersec
content: Yes, in terms of numbers that doesn't look too exquisitely fine-tuned, 500ms seems broadly reasonable

author: potuz
content:
<@592004585506340885> <@199561711278227457> <@363800010518822915> an issue arose in Prysm that was found by Pari and leads me to think that the spec may need to be a little more verbose about bid forwarding over P2P. Perhaps no action is needed but let me describe the problem I can think of. 

Suppose that clients do a naive DOS prevention of the form: I only forward the highest paying valid bid. Then attackers can send a high paying bid for empty when the chain is overwhelmingly seeing full. The bid is valid, but no proposer will take it and this can eclipse good bids. 

Prysm seems to filter by "highest paying for a combination blocroot/blockhash"  which solves this. Another option would be for nodes to only forward bids compatible with their own head view. 

At any rate, perhaps a little sentence regarding these possible issues may be useful.

author: potuz
content:
> proposers are already side stepping the current 1 second timeout by just calling produce block later into the slot and playing timing games that way.
Yes, this is unavoidable and I don' t really mind it if there is slack in the slot, there' s no reason for neither the proposer or the builder not to take it. What I want is that the decision here is not done by an external agent that holds a lock on every single request, including unrelated ones. The proposer should be the one deciding the deadline here. Ask late if you want, and send the block with your favorite bid. Instead you may be asking earlier (unavoidable if you set the deadline larger) and sending late a bid that you actually got early, but had to anyway continue waiting unnecessarily.

author: l03443
content:
> What I want is that the decision here is not done by an external agent
What is the goal here? Last time we looked ~70% of proposers stick with the default settings for timeouts even though they could increase it easily and have better rewards. By setting a relatively "worse" default and creating a burden to tune timings you are potentially putting unsophisticated proposers at a disadvantage

author: potuz
content: We can agree on a larger default for asking: the goal here is clear move the timing game decision away from a relay and into the node operator now that it can take it. We want to squeeze timing games as much as possible so that we can squeeze the attestation deadline as much as possible so that we can have a lower dead time of the slot as much as possible so that we can have a larger gas limit and a higher blob count.

author: l03443
content: How does moving the timing game decision away from the relay help in squeezing the attestation deadling? The relay takes a number of factors in consideration including the deadline itself

author: potuz
content: If it were a single relay then it would be fine, but now the node contacts multiple of them, and anyone of them has control over every other requests, making a race to the bottom.

author: l03443
content: How? The timeout is always < attestation deadline, if the deadline is reduced at the consensus level the some relays will just time out? But again no relay would delay past the deadline

author: nflaig
content: I have a question on testing related to

author: jtraglia
content:
Hey there. So what Prysm does is exactly how [the spec is written](https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/blob/e310d1c0d316e4dcc822164966c5bfa592adae2b/specs/gloas/p2p-interface.md?plain=1#L432-L433):
> - _[IGNORE]_ this bid is the highest value bid seen for the tuple `(bid.slot, bid.parent_block_hash, bid.parent_block_root)`.

author: potuz
content: yeah, I was mostly concerned with the anti-DOS features, but yeah, the filter being by hash/root is good enough

author: potuz
content: Terence made me notice this earlier

author: potuz
content: The issue we had was a bit more brutal though 🙂 we were validating the bid with the incorrect block hash lol

author: alextes
content: One thing i'm not entirely clear on: is this discussion only about how long a CL waits for a reply? (and you want to fix it to always 300ms or 500ms, no config). Are you then also saying operators will get a config parameter to control how deep into the slot the request is fired? Making the bid reply deadline (this is what matters most to us) slot_start (protocol controls) + bid_request_delay (protocol sets max 3s, proposer controls 0 - 3s, relay / builder utilizes maximally) + bid_request_timeout (CL dev controls, 300 - 500ms, relay / builder utilizes maximally)?

author: alextes
content: And then what does this mean for a proposer who would like to call say three times? Still fine, just all requests would have the CL controlled timeout?

author: potuz
content: what do you mean a proposer who would like to call three times? if any clients provides that feature, that's definitely not part of what we are planning to specify or standardize

author: alextes
content: a proposer asking for a bid three times. so that they may balance getting a high late bid, with having any bid better than the local block at all. imo fine if CLs don't provide this as long as side-cars can. but that discussion deserves its own thread maybe  😅 .

author: serenita_io
content: Is there a good reason to want to call three times? Why not just once with a large delay? That way you still get the same late bid?

author: serenita_io
content: The node operator is in full control over the full timing as long as they set the `Date-Milliseconds` + `X-Timeout-Ms` headers. Why not make them required and call it a day?

author: potuz
content: why would you want to call with a large delay instead of calling later?

author: serenita_io
content: It simplifies things. You simply call at the start of a slot and expect to receive a response by your deadline/timeout.

author: potuz
content: If anything I see more of a usage to what <@177711679977684993> says of calling twice with a short deadline instead of once with a larger delay

author: potuz
content: how is it different than calling at your deadline/timeout time with zero delay?

author: serenita_io
content: In this case you need to account for latency to the various relays you connect to

author: potuz
content: how isn' t this latency also not at play with the deadline?

author: serenita_io
content:
If you call at the start of slot and tell all relays to get a response back to you by 1s into the slot, they can all calculate how far into the slot they need to send their response back (by the headers). All responses will be back to you by ~1s into the slot.

If you call say 500ms into the slot with a 0 timeout, a close relay will return a bid by 510ms whereas a relay further away returns a bid by 600ms into the slot.

author: potuz
content: that's not how the deadline works though, you aren' t passing an absolute value but rather a timeout from the time of the call.

author: serenita_io
content:
Unless I'm missing something, it is effectively how the combination of the two headers work.

You set the `Date-Milliseconds` to the actual time that you make the request, and `X-Timeout-Ms` header to the time you're willing to wait for a response. Based on those two headers, the relay can tell roughly how much latency there is and time its response.

author: potuz
content: Then you can do the exact same thing just sending a late request...

author: data.always
content:
~~there's not really any reason to do this. you could just call get_header more times if you wanted the absolute latest request possible. usually what proposers are trying to do with multiple requests is adverse select builders/searchers when cex prices move last second and the auction value drops.~~

edit: nvm you're talking about post-epbs. i am dumb

author: serenita_io
content:
You can, but if you send a late request with a 0 timeout, you're going to get responses back at random times, putting builders closer to you at a disadvantage.

I really don't get why anyone would want to complicate things. Simply call at the start of the slot and inform the builder by what time you want to have a response back. Easy. No downside.

author: data.always
content: i'm broadly aligned with shortening the deadline and moving the ability to tune deadlines into clients (forked clients are bad, most proposers will still use defaults). i think 300ms is probably the lower end of fine, even for auckland that would let you source bids from Tokyo/NA/EU and still be competitive + have CR. how much value is there in making sure we can support the worst latency path on the planet? it feels like returns diminish quickly imo.

author: tersec
content: Communicating this level of vaguely knowable nuance to end-users is going to be a consistent support problem

author: tersec
content: People generally go use a few specific guides, or some other defaults, and might or might not think about or realize that a very tight timeout is going to create issues

author: potuz
content: I think it's actually the opposite here, and that's why my reply above to <@654267572107083777>. In fact we help those proposers by actually making the deadline very short and having them choose on time from the p2p bids. We *could* enable external calls from areas where the ping time is higher say than the attestation deadline by sending the request earlier than the slot time for example.

author: tersec
content: So set up the non-P2P bid system to notionally work but be handicapped in a subtle way? I don't even really care if the latter exists, personally, but if it works it should work well and reliably and this seems to almost be a deliberate effort to make it flaky to direct people to the P2P system

author: potuz
content: you are handicapped if you are proposing from a remote area and are already planning on adding latency to your proposal path. Making the default wait time longer does not help you

author: potuz
content: Sure, perhaps 300ms is too low, which is the reason why I **want** to agree on some sane default, I don't want to die on a number, but I will stress out that this number needs to be the minimum possible: adding dead time to every single block is absolutely crazy

author: ethdreamer
content: <@911055007837720597> do you know how much time a builder needs (beyond the round trip time) to respond to the request?

author: ethdreamer
content: Because technically the RTT is measurable by the BN ahead of time when we send the `BuilderPreferences` or even if we just hit `/eth/v1/builder/status` ahead of time..

author: potuz
content: I have numbers from Titan and Ultrasound from a few months ago and it's of the order of milliseconds.  However, you'd need to add a signature verification for those builders that care about the signed auth

author: potuz
content: But also the problem is the actions that need to be taken: in the current approach the block can be sealed *after* receviving the request. But in Gloas the block could be required to be sealed before. The builder needs to keep the latest viable block and can/may be forced to return the best block it has JIT

author: ethdreamer
content:
> in the current approach the block can be sealed after receviving the request. But in Gloas the block could be required to be sealed before. 
I'm not sure I understand what you mean by this - what do you mean by seal?

author: potuz
content: finish the block and compute the state root

author: data.always
content: if relays are popular then it's trivial, building will happen ahead of time to facilitate price discovery (at our relay i think it's a couple ms to respond). if direct connections are popular then what should be most important is having accurate predictions of when bids need to be delivered + latency (if the timeout is long enough, builders can keep building and seal later--if the timeout is short, then builders can seal ahead of time).

author: data.always
content: imo the best thing to do is to have the end time of the auction explicitly communicated to builders/relays. right now, relays don't explicitly know when the auction is going to end, so it becomes a game of measuring latencies and deanonymizing proposers to predict when that bid needs to be returned, which seems undesirable.

author: ethdreamer
content:
> imo the best thing to do is to have the end time of the auction explicitly communicated to builders/relays
So you just mean ensuring the clients pass `Date-Milliseconds` and `X-Timeout-Ms`?

author: vladimir_ea
content:
Is it correct that the relays control ‘the timing games decision’? How do they do that?

Currently the proposer sends a getHeader request specifying a timeout. If a relay returns beyond that timeout their bid is not considered. The timeout decision is already entirely the proposer’s. 

Certainly relays participate in this by trying to honour the timeouts but the decision on timing is already the proposer’s not the relay’s.
